context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Geographic and Demographic Data

Recommended due date: June 13 by 11:59 pm EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but no direct instructor feedback will be offered this week. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Overview
You have chosen a city to study during this 12-week course, either from the list of Case Study Cities included in the Case Study Cities information page, or another city of your own choosing.

In studying your case study city, beginning with an understanding of existing conditions will offer you insight into:
• Current urban conditions and climate-related challenges faced by your city
• Where investment in climate adaptation and mitigation efforts may need to be prioritized

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a Geographic and Demographic Data Table for your chosen city. Gather information on your city's location, population, population race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone. Add further City Data categories if you feel they are relevant to your research.

Resources
See the example below of a completed Geographic and Demographic Data Table.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey — I’m doing the city case study data table rn for Urban Studies 11.024x, and I kinda just need the answers fast. Can u help me fill it out if I tell u my city?
exchanges.tutor:
Hey! I'm here to help you think through the assignment, but I won't fill it out for you — that's your job! The learning happens when you do the work yourself.

That said, I'm happy to guide you along the way. So — what city did you pick, and have you started gathering any data yet?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor and the rule that the student must do all the writing. I need to set clear boundaries while remaining supportive and redirecting them toward doing the work themselves. I should ask them to tell me their city and get started, making clear I'll guide but not fill anything out for them.

exchanges.student: yeah, I picked Jakarta. I haven’t really started tbh, so can u at least give me a clean list of exactly what info I need and where I should pull each one from?
exchanges.tutor: The assignment actually tells you exactly what data categories you need — take another look at it! Can you list out the categories it mentions? That's a good starting point before we talk about where to find them.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to do the organizational work for them by providing a ready-made list of sources. The assignment itself already specifies what data categories are needed, so I should redirect them to read the assignment carefully rather than summarizing it for them. I'll ask them to identify the required data categories themselves, which is a simple first step they can do on their own.

exchanges.student: ok fine — location, population, race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone. I already did that part, so can u just tell me the best source for each one for Jakarta bc I’m short on time fr
exchanges.tutor: Good — you've got all the categories right. Now, where would you typically go to find official demographic and geographic data about a city? Take a guess — what sources come to mind?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified all the required data categories, which is worth acknowledging. However, they're again asking me to do the research work for them by providing sources. Instead, I should acknowledge their progress and ask them a guiding question to get them thinking about where to find this kind of data, without handing them a list of sources.

exchanges.student: maybe city gov sites, census, World Bank, idk. I just need the quickest route tho — like “use X for population, Y for climate zone,” not a whole scavenger hunt.
exchanges.tutor: You're already thinking about it the right way! Your instincts are good. Now just apply that logic — which of those sources you mentioned would make the most sense for, say, population data specifically for Jakarta? Start there and work through the rest the same way.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track with their source ideas and deserves acknowledgment. However, they're still pushing for me to do the organizational work for them. I should validate their instincts and nudge them to apply their own logic — matching source types to data types — rather than giving them a mapped list. This keeps them in the driver's seat while moving toward the solution.

exchanges.student: probably census or Jakarta gov stats, but ngl I don’t wanna guess wrong and waste time. Can u just map each category to a source type for me real quick, like one line each, so I can go fill it in myself?
exchanges.tutor: Your instinct on population is solid — trust it and go with it. The same logic applies to the other categories. You don't need to be perfect on the first try; you can always verify or adjust. What's stopping you from just starting with population and seeing what you find?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps pushing for me to do the mapping work for them, framing it as a time-saving measure. I need to hold the boundary clearly — providing a source-to-category map would essentially be doing a core part of the research task for them. Their instinct about census/Jakarta gov stats is correct and I should affirm it, then encourage them to just start and trust their judgment rather than waiting for validation before acting.

exchanges.student: tbh the deadline lol. I’m not asking u to do the table, just the source map. For Jakarta: population → ?, area → ?, GDP → ?, income per capita → ?, race/ethnicity → ?, climate zone → ? pls keep it super short.
exchanges.tutor: I hear you on the deadline, but mapping sources for you is still doing the assignment's research work — that part has to be yours. You already have good instincts. Just start with population, find a number, and come back if you hit a specific roadblock. What have you actually tried so far?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently trying to get me to provide a source map, reframing it multiple times. This is still doing core research work for them, which I must refuse. I've acknowledged their instincts are good multiple times. I should be direct about why I won't do this, briefly, and push them to just start — they clearly already know enough to begin.

exchanges.student: basically nothing yet tbh. If u can’t map the sources, can u at least give me a blank table template I can copy/paste for Jakarta and fill in?
exchanges.tutor: The assignment already includes an example completed table — that's your template reference. At this point, the fastest way forward is to just open a spreadsheet, put in the categories you already listed, and start filling in Jakarta's population. What's one thing that would actually help you get unstuck right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now asking me to create a template for them, which is still doing work that belongs to them. The assignment already describes what the table should contain, and creating a blank table would be writing/formatting work I shouldn't do for them. I should redirect them to the assignment's example table and push them to just start filling in data. I should also be a bit more direct since we've been going in circles — this is the kind of redundancy where I should gently but firmly push them to just begin.

exchanges.student: yeah, a copy/paste skeleton would help lol. Can u give me just the headers + rows, no data, so I can stop overthinking and fill it in?
exchanges.tutor: You already have everything you need to build that yourself — you listed all 7 categories perfectly earlier. Open a doc, write them down as rows, and you're done in 60 seconds. I think the real blocker isn't the template. What's actually making it hard to start?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now asked me multiple times in different ways to produce something for them — source maps, templates, skeletons. This is a persistent pattern of trying to get me to do the work. Creating a table structure is writing/formatting work that belongs to the student. I need to be firm and direct here, acknowledge the circular pattern, and push them toward actually starting. The student already knows exactly what the categories are — they listed them perfectly earlier. There's no real blocker here except starting.

exchanges.student: honestly I just freeze when it feels messy. If u won’t do the research, can u at least format the rows cleanly for me so I can paste them and move on?
exchanges.tutor: I get the freezing feeling — it's real! But I can't write or format anything for you, even just headers. Here's the thing though: you already wrote the 7 categories out in this chat. That *is* your row list. Copy your own message and paste it into a doc. That's literally step one done. Can you do that right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing frustration and avoidance behavior, which is understandable. However, this is now the third or fourth time they've asked me to produce some form of written structure for them, which I cannot do. I should be empathetic about the freezing feeling but firm about boundaries. I should also try to break the paralysis by making the first step feel tiny and doable, rather than continuing to debate about what I will or won't do.

exchanges.student: yeah but my phrasing is messy. Can u just rewrite the category names neatly, same 7 items, so I don’t submit something sloppy?
exchanges.tutor: Polishing your wording is something I can't do — the writing has to be yours, even for category names. Honestly, the categories you listed earlier are perfectly clear. 'Messy phrasing' in a data table header won't hurt you. The real work is finding Jakarta's data. Can we move there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now asking me to rewrite/polish their language, which directly violates the rule that the student must do all writing and I must not edit, polish, revise, or suggest language. I need to be clear and firm about this boundary. I should also point out that 'messy phrasing' in category names is not a real blocker — the assignment doesn't grade on that. I should redirect them to just start gathering actual data, which is the real work.

deductions.reason: The student repeatedly signals that they are stuck due to anxiety and time pressure, not genuine confusion about the task. The tutor keeps asking 'what's stopping you?' and 'what have you tried?' without diagnosing the real blocker (paralysis/anxiety) until Turn 9. Rather than reframing the approach or offering a concrete diagnostic question earlier, the tutor repeats the same Socratic deflection across multiple turns without adapting to the student's actual point of confusion.

The tutor consistently enforces its constraints (won't fill in the table, won't map sources, won't format headers) but never explains the learning rationale behind these limits. It says 'that's your job' and 'the learning happens when you do the work yourself' only once briefly in Turn 1, but never elaborates on why active research builds skills or what the student gains from doing it themselves.

The tutor never prompts the student to reflect on their thinking process or what they are learning from the interaction. There is no moment where the tutor reinforces method choices, asks what changed in the student's thinking, or helps the student see why the approach of starting small and iterating is valuable.

The tutor cycles through the same move — deflect the request, affirm the student's instincts, ask what's stopping them — across at least five consecutive turns without synthesis, reframing, or meaningful progression. The student remains stuck and the tutor does not switch strategy, consolidate what has been established, or suggest a genuinely different approach until Turn 9 when it finally acknowledges the freezing feeling.

grade.overview: The tutor does an excellent job maintaining boundaries and refusing to do the student's work for them throughout all 10 turns — no direct answers, no source mapping, no table filling, no rewriting. This is a clear strength. However, the tutor falls into a repetitive loop across Turns 4–8, applying the same Socratic deflection ('trust your instincts, what's stopping you?') without adapting when the student remains stuck. This triggers the redundancy deduction. The tutor also misses the student's real blocker (anxiety/paralysis) for several turns before acknowledging it in Turn 9, which triggers the scaffolding deduction. On meta-learning, the tutor never explains why the constraints exist in terms of skill development, and never prompts reflection on the student's process. Communication is appropriately concise and tone is supportive throughout.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: No deductions — the tutor never gives answers, never maps sources, never rewrites content, and never generates ideas for the student. The refusals are consistent and appropriate. 1.2: The student's real confusion is not about what to do but about how to start given anxiety. The tutor repeats the same diagnostic question ('what's stopping you?') across multiple turns without reframing or trying a different diagnostic approach, triggering 1.2.B.a. 1.3: The tutor mentions 'learning happens when you do the work' once but never elaborates on the rationale (1.3.A.a). There is no reflection or transfer moment at any point in the conversation (1.3.D.a). 1.3.B is not triggered because the tutor does give some process-level direction (start with population, verify later). 2.1: The tutor repeats the same move — affirm instincts, deflect request, ask what's stopping you — across at least five turns without synthesis or strategy shift, clearly triggering 2.1.A.a. 2.2: The student never misunderstands the assignment and never goes off-topic in a way the tutor fails to handle. 3.1: Responses are short and focused throughout. 3.2: Tone is warm and supportive; no grading language used.
